attrs.alt: PIM approval workflow: request → MFA + justification → pending → approver decision → active or denied

content.text: When to Require Approval

Not every role needs an approval workflow — adding approval to low-risk roles creates friction without proportional security gain. Reserve approval for roles where unauthorized access would cause significant harm.
Role / Scope
Recommended Approval Requirement
Justification
Owner at subscription scope
Required — single approver
Full resource control; high blast radius
Contributor at production RG
Required — single approver
Can delete or misconfigure production resources
Contributor at dev/test RG
Not required
Lower impact; developer productivity more important
Reader anywhere
Not required
Read-only; minimal harm from unauthorized access
Global Administrator (Entra)
Required — two-stage approval
Highest-privilege Entra role; tenant-wide impact
User Administrator (Entra)
Required — single approver
Can modify users and reset passwords
Security Reader (Entra)
Not required
Read-only security data
Custom role at resource scope
Based on actions included
Evaluate the role definition's blast radius

A good heuristic: if the role can delete, modify security settings, or access sensitive data at a broad scope, require approval. If the role only reads data or performs limited writes, MFA + justification is sufficient.
Reading the Current Approval Policy

PIM approval settings are stored in roleManagementPolicy objects associated with each role-scope combination. A single subscription can have different approval settings for Owner versus Contributor versus Reader.

# Read the current PIM approval policy for a role at a scope
$scope    = '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
$roleDefId = (Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Owner').Id
$token    = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token
$headers  = @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token" }

# List all role management policies at the scope
$policies = Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET -Headers $headers \
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleManagementPolicies?api-version=2020-10-01"

# Find the policy for the Owner role
# The policy-to-role mapping is found in roleManagementPolicyAssignments
$policyAssignments = Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET -Headers $headers \
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleManagementPolicyAssignments?api-version=2020-10-01"

$ownerAssignment = $policyAssignments.value | Where-Object {
    $_.properties.roleDefinitionId -like "*$roleDefId*"
}
$policyId = $ownerAssignment.properties.policyId

# Get the full policy (includes all rules: approval, MFA, notification, etc.)
$policy = Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET -Headers $headers \
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleManagementPolicies/$($policyId.Split('/')[-1])?api-version=2020-10-01"

# Find and display the approval rule
$approvalRule = $policy.properties.rules | Where-Object { $_.id -eq 'Approval_EndUser_Assignment' }
$approvalRule | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 10
Enabling Approval with an Approver Group

Approval configuration is a PATCH to the roleManagementPolicy's Approval_EndUser_Assignment rule. You specify the approver group, the timeout window, and whether approvers must provide justification for their decision.

# Update PIM policy to require approval with a specific approver group
# This patches only the approval rule within the policy

$scope      = '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
$token      = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token
$headers    = @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token"; 'Content-Type' = 'application/json' }

# (Get policyId using the read script above)
$policyId   = 'xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx'  # from previous query
$approverGroupId = (Get-AzADGroup -DisplayName 'pim-approvers-production').Id

# Build the updated approval rule
$approvalRulePatch = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.unifiedRoleManagementPolicyApprovalRule'
    id            = 'Approval_EndUser_Assignment'
    setting       = @{
        isApprovalRequired              = $true
        isApprovalRequiredForExtension  = $false
        isRequestorJustificationRequired = $true
        approvalMode                    = 'SingleStage'
        approvalStages                  = @(
            @{
                approvalStageTimeOutInDays      = 1    # 24-hour approval window
                isApproverJustificationRequired = $true
                escalationTimeInMinutes         = 0
                isEscalationEnabled             = $false
                primaryApprovers                = @(
                    @{
                        '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.groupMembers'
                        groupId       = $approverGroupId
                        description   = 'Production PIM approvers'
                    }
                )
            }
        )
    }
}

# PATCH the specific rule in the policy (not a full policy replacement)
$patchBody = @{ properties = @{ rules = @($approvalRulePatch) } } | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 15
Invoke-RestMethod -Method PATCH -Headers $headers -Body $patchBody \
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleManagementPolicies/$policyId?api-version=2020-10-01"
The approver group should be a security group in Entra ID. All members of the group can approve or deny requests. Add enough members so approvals are timely even when individuals are unavailable.
Set approvalStageTimeOutInDays to 1 (24 hours) for most roles. For urgent operational roles, consider using a shorter timeout and ensuring the approver group is staffed 24/7.
isApproverJustificationRequired = true forces approvers to explain their decision. This creates an audit trail of WHY access was granted, not just THAT it was granted.
The approver group membership itself should be PIM-eligible — approvers should not be approvers permanently if they are themselves privileged users.
Two-Stage Approval for Critical Roles

For the highest-sensitivity roles (Global Administrator, subscription Owner in regulated environments), a two-stage approval workflow provides an additional check. Stage 1 typically involves the requester's manager; Stage 2 involves a security review.

# Two-stage approval for the highest-privilege roles (e.g. Global Administrator)
# Stage 1: direct manager approves
# Stage 2: security team reviews after manager approval

$managersGroupId  = (Get-AzADGroup -DisplayName 'direct-managers').Id
$securityGroupId  = (Get-AzADGroup -DisplayName 'security-approvers').Id

$twoStageApproval = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.unifiedRoleManagementPolicyApprovalRule'
    id            = 'Approval_EndUser_Assignment'
    setting       = @{
        isApprovalRequired = $true
        approvalMode       = 'Serial'   # stages run sequentially
        approvalStages     = @(
            @{  # Stage 1: manager
                approvalStageTimeOutInDays = 1
                isApproverJustificationRequired = $true
                primaryApprovers = @(@{ '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.groupMembers'; groupId = $managersGroupId })
            },
            @{  # Stage 2: security team
                approvalStageTimeOutInDays = 1
                isApproverJustificationRequired = $true
                primaryApprovers = @(@{ '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.groupMembers'; groupId = $securityGroupId })
            }
        )
    }
}
# PATCH this to the policy as shown in the previous code block
Serial mode: stages run sequentially — stage 1 must approve before stage 2 is notified.
Each stage has its own timeout window and approver group.
Two-stage approval works best when the two sets of approvers have different perspectives: business justification (manager) and security assessment (security team).
The entire approval chain must complete within the combined timeout window. Configure the activation request timeout to be at least the sum of both stage timeouts.
Viewing and Processing Pending Approvals

Approvers can view pending requests in the PIM portal under 'Approve requests', via the PIM notification email, or via API. Time-sensitive operational roles benefit from additional notification channels beyond email.

# Approver: list pending PIM approval requests
# Azure resource role approvals
$scope  = '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
$token  = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token

# List pending requests that need approval (filter: status = PendingApproval)
$pending = Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET `
    -Headers @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token" } `
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignmentScheduleRequests?api-version=2020-10-01&`$filter=status eq 'PendingApproval'"

$pending.value | Select-Object `
    @{n='RequestId';e={$_.name}},
    @{n='Requester';e={$_.properties.principalId}},
    @{n='Role';e={$_.properties.expandedProperties.roleDefinition.displayName}},
    @{n='Scope';e={$_.properties.scope}},
    @{n='Justification';e={$_.properties.justification}},
    @{n='RequestedDuration';e={$_.properties.scheduleInfo.expiration.duration}}

# For Entra role approvals, use Microsoft Graph:
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'RoleManagementPolicy.Read.Directory','RoleAssignmentSchedule.ReadWrite.Directory'
Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignmentScheduleRequest -Filter "status eq 'PendingApproval'"

# Approver: approve a pending PIM request via REST
$scope     = '/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
$token     = (Get-AzAccessToken).Token
$requestId = 'xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx'  # from pending list

# Get the approval decision object for this request
$approval = Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET `
    -Headers @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token" } `
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignmentApprovals/$requestId?api-version=2021-01-01-preview"

$approvalStageId = $approval.properties.steps[0].id

# Submit approval decision
$decisionBody = @{
    reviewResult = 'Approve'  # or 'Deny'
    justification = 'Verified incident INC-4892 in ServiceNow. Change window approved.'
} | ConvertTo-Json

Invoke-RestMethod -Method PUT `
    -Headers @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token"; 'Content-Type' = 'application/json' } `
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com$scope/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignmentApprovals/$requestId/stages/$approvalStageId?api-version=2021-01-01-preview" `
    -Body $decisionBody

Write-Host "Approval submitted. The requester will be notified and their role will be activated."
Integrating with Microsoft Teams

Approvers who work primarily in Teams may miss email notifications. A Logic App or Power Automate flow can post PIM approval requests to a Teams channel, where approvers can click a deep link to the PIM portal and take action.

# Integrate PIM approvals with Microsoft Teams via Power Automate
# Pattern: when a PIM approval request is received, post to a Teams channel
# so approvers see it in their work channel rather than only in email.

# Power Automate trigger: 'When a HTTP request is received' or use
# Entra PIM notification webhook (preview) → Logic App → Teams connector

# Alternative: Logic App with timer polling PIM pending approvals
# (runs every 5 minutes, posts new pending requests to Teams)

# Logic App pseudo-flow:
# Trigger: Recurrence (every 5 minutes)
# Action 1: Get access token (Managed Identity)
# Action 2: HTTP GET /roleAssignmentScheduleRequests?$filter=status eq 'PendingApproval'
# Condition: if value count > 0 (new pending requests)
# Action 3: For each pending request:
#   Post adaptive card to Teams channel with:
#     - Requester name
#     - Role and scope
#     - Justification
#     - Approve/Deny buttons (deep link to PIM portal)

# Teams Adaptive Card deep link format for PIM approval:
$pimApprovalUrl = 'https://entra.microsoft.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_PIMCommon/ApprovalDetailBlade/approvalId/<approval-id>'
Write-Host "Approvers can click this URL from Teams to approve: $pimApprovalUrl"
ITSM Integration: Ticket-Linked Approvals

Many organizations require changes to production systems to be tracked in an ITSM tool (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management). PIM's ticket information fields capture the ITSM ticket number at activation time, enabling approvers to cross-reference before approving.

# Integrate PIM with ServiceNow via ticket validation
# Pattern: requester must provide a valid open ServiceNow ticket number.
# An approver (or automated Logic App) validates the ticket before approving.

# The PIM ticketInfo field captures the ticket number at activation time:
# users must enter: ticket system = 'ServiceNow', ticket number = 'INC-12345'

# Automated validation Logic App flow:
# Trigger: When PIM request arrives (via webhook or polling)
# Action 1: Extract ticketNumber from PIM request body
# Action 2: GET /api/now/table/incident?number=<ticketNumber> (ServiceNow API)
# Condition: If ticket exists AND state = 'In Progress' AND assigned_to = requester
#   True:  Auto-approve the PIM request via ARM API
#   False: Auto-deny with comment explaining why

# Manual validation (approver verifies ticket before clicking Approve):
# PIM email includes ticketNumber in the notification
# Approver opens ServiceNow, verifies the ticket, then approves in PIM

# Example ServiceNow API call to validate ticket (from approver's script):
$ticketNumber = 'INC-12345'
$snow_url = "https://contoso.service-now.com/api/now/table/incident"
$snow_headers = @{
    Authorization = "Basic $(([System.Convert]::ToBase64String([System.Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetBytes('user:password'))))"
    Accept = 'application/json'
}
Invoke-RestMethod -Method GET -Headers $snow_headers \
    -Uri "$snow_url?sysparm_query=number=$ticketNumber&sysparm_fields=number,state,assigned_to,short_description" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty result
Integration Level
What it requires
Benefit
Manual validation
Approver looks up ticket manually before approving
Zero setup; works with any ITSM
Automated validation (Logic App)
Logic App polls PIM + queries ITSM API; auto-approves if ticket is valid
Faster approvals; consistent validation
Full ITSM-driven JIT
ITSM change approval triggers PIM activation automatically
Fully automated; no separate PIM approval step
Auditing Approval Decisions

Every approval decision — who approved, when, what they wrote as justification — is recorded in the Entra audit log. Regular analysis of this data catches rubber-stamp approvers and reveals patterns that warrant policy changes.

# Audit approved and denied PIM requests for compliance reporting
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All'

# Get PIM approval decisions from the last 30 days
$start = (Get-Date).AddDays(-30).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')

# Entra role approvals
$approvals = Get-MgAuditLogDirectoryAudit -Filter \
    "activityDisplayName eq 'Approve request to activate member in role (PIM activation)' and activityDateTime ge $start"

$report = $approvals | Select-Object ActivityDateTime,
    @{n='Approver';e={$_.InitiatedBy.User.UserPrincipalName}},
    @{n='Requester';e={($_.TargetResources | Where-Object Type -eq 'User').UserPrincipalName}},
    @{n='Role';e={($_.TargetResources | Where-Object Type -eq 'Role').DisplayName}},
    @{n='Result';e={$_.Result}}

$report | Export-Csv -Path './pim-approval-audit.csv' -NoTypeInformation

# Identify approvers who approve 100% of requests (rubber-stamp approvers)
$report | Group-Object Approver | ForEach-Object {
    $approvals = ($_.Group | Where-Object Result -eq 'success').Count
    $denials   = ($_.Group | Where-Object Result -ne 'success').Count
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        Approver    = $_.Name
        TotalReview = $_.Count
        Approved    = $approvals
        Denied      = $denials
        ApprovalRate = "$(([math]::Round($approvals/$_.Count*100)))%"
    }
} | Sort-Object TotalReview -Descending | Format-Table -AutoSize
An approver with 100% approval rate on more than 10 requests is a red flag — either every request is legitimate (unlikely) or the approver is rubber-stamping without review.
Analyze approval latency: if approvals consistently happen within 1 minute of the request, the approver may not be actually reviewing the justification.
Denials are valuable signals. Track denial reasons and feedback them into user education to reduce invalid request submissions.
Schedule this audit report to run monthly and distribute it to the CISO and security team as part of the access governance review.
Common Approval Workflow Pitfalls
Pitfall
Impact
Mitigation
Single approver with no backup
Requests blocked when approver is on vacation
Use a group of 3+ approvers; include an on-call rotation
24+ hour timeout with no on-call
Production incidents stalled waiting for approval
Set short timeout for Ops roles; designate 24/7 on-call approver
Approvers in the same team as requesters
Peer pressure leads to rubber-stamp approvals
Use cross-team approvers: manager + security team
No approval justification required
No audit evidence of why access was granted
Always require approver justification
Approval group has PIM-eligible roles
Approver must activate own role before they can approve
Approver group members hold active (permanent low-priv) roles
Approval for all roles including Reader
Excessive friction; users bypass PIM
Reserve approval for high-risk roles only
Summary
Require PIM approval only for roles with high blast radius — Owner, production Contributor, Global Administrator. Low-risk roles need only MFA and justification.
Approval is configured per-role-per-scope via the roleManagementPolicy REST API. Different subscriptions and different roles can have different approval requirements.
Always use an approver group, not an individual. Set a timeout and require approver justification to prevent rubber-stamping.
Two-stage approval (manager → security team) provides stronger controls for tenant-wide admin roles.
Integrate approval notifications with Teams or Slack to ensure approvers see requests in their primary work channel.
ITSM ticket validation — manual or automated — connects PIM approvals to change management processes.
Audit approval decisions monthly. High approval rates without denials, or near-instant approvals, are indicators of process failure.
